title: Quiz: Cooling Stages
description: Test your understanding of temperature stages, thermal budgets, and heat management in dilution refrigerator cryostats

questions.question: Which temperature stage in a typical dilution refrigerator has the SMALLEST cooling power capacity?

options.text: 50 K stage (pulse tube first stage)
4 K stage (pulse tube second stage)
Still stage (~700 mK)
Mixing chamber (~10-20 mK)

questions.explanation: The mixing chamber has the smallest cooling power, typically only 10-25 uW at base temperature. Compare this to ~30W at 50K, ~1.5W at 4K, and ~20mW at the still. This is why protecting the mixing chamber from heat loads is critical.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the 50 K radiation shield?

options.text: To block magnetic fields from reaching the qubits
To intercept thermal radiation from room temperature before it reaches the 4K stage
To provide mechanical support for the cryostat
To thermalize the 3He gas before it enters the dilution unit

questions.explanation: The 50K shield intercepts thermal radiation from the 300K vacuum can. Without this shield, the ~11W of radiation would overwhelm the 4K stage which only has ~1.5W cooling capacity. The 50K stage can handle this load easily with its ~30W capacity.

questions.question: Why is the 4 K stage often considered the 'bottleneck' in cryostat design?

options.text: It is the coldest stage in the system
It has limited cooling power (~1.5W) while needing to support HEMTs, wiring, and heat from 50K
It is where the dilution process occurs
It has the largest physical size

questions.explanation: The 4K stage has limited cooling power (~1.5W) but must handle multiple heat sources: conducted heat from the 50K stage, HEMT amplifier dissipation, wiring thermalization, and radiation. Poor heat budget management here can prevent reaching base temperature.

questions.question: What happens at the 'still' stage of a dilution refrigerator?

options.text: 3He and 4He mix together for the first time
3He is evaporated from the dilute phase due to its higher vapor pressure
The qubit chip is mounted and measured
Liquid nitrogen is used to precool incoming helium

questions.explanation: At the still (~700mK), 3He is evaporated from the dilute phase because 3He has much higher vapor pressure than 4He at this temperature. Pumping on the still removes 3He, driving the dilution cycle.

questions.question: Copper has higher thermal conductivity at 4 K than at room temperature.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! Pure copper (especially OFHC copper) has much higher thermal conductivity at 4K than at 300K. This is because electron-phonon scattering decreases at low temperatures. This property makes copper excellent for heat spreading at cryogenic temperatures.

questions.question: You need to run a wire from room temperature to the mixing chamber. How should it be thermalized?

options.text: Run it directly to the mixing chamber with no intermediate connections
Thermalize at 50K, 4K, still, and cold plate stages before reaching the mixing chamber
Only thermalize at the 4K stage
Use thick copper wire to maximize heat transfer to room temperature

questions.explanation: Proper thermalization requires anchoring the wire at every temperature stage. Each stage intercepts heat conducted from warmer regions, preventing it from reaching colder stages. Running directly to the mixing chamber would overwhelm its tiny cooling power.

questions.question: Which of the following are typically mounted at the 4 K stage? (Select all that apply)

options.text: HEMT amplifiers for qubit readout
Circulators and isolators
Infrared blocking filters
The qubit chip itself

questions.explanation: The 4K stage hosts HEMT amplifiers (which operate optimally at 4K), circulators/isolators (which need to be cold but can dissipate some power), and IR filters (critical for blocking thermal radiation). The qubit chip is mounted at the mixing chamber (~10-20mK).
